description:
You are acting as an on-call incident responder.  
    A system you are investigating already contains a small CSV log of failed
    authentication attempts:

        /home/user/logs/failed_logins.csv

    Its exact, current contents are:

        user,timestamp,ip,reason
        alice,2023-08-21T12:05:34Z,192.168.1.45,invalid_password
        bob,2023-08-21T12:06:02Z,192.168.1.45,invalid_password
        carol,2023-08-21T12:10:11Z,10.0.0.3,account_locked
        dan,2023-08-21T12:11:47Z,10.0.0.3,invalid_password
        erin,2023-08-21T12:12:33Z,192.168.1.100,invalid_password
        frank,2023-08-21T12:13:01Z,10.0.0.3,invalid_password

    Your investigation requires two artefacts to be produced from this file:

    1) A **filtered CSV** that contains ONLY the rows whose “reason” column has the
       exact value “invalid_password”.  Save it as

           /home/user/analysis/invalid_password_attempts.csv

       Formatting rules the grader will check:
         • The file must include the header line exactly as in the source CSV.  
         • The order of the columns must remain “user,timestamp,ip,reason”.  
         • Preserve the original row order from the source when writing the
           filtered rows.  
         • Terminate the file with a single trailing newline (Unix “\n”).

    2) A **JSON summary** that counts how many “invalid_password” events each IP
       address generated.  Save it as

           /home/user/analysis/invalid_password_summary.json

       Required JSON format:
         • Top-level value must be an object (i.e. { … }).
         • Each key is an IP address string, each value is an integer count.  
         • The keys **must appear in descending numeric count** order.  If two IPs
           have the same count, preserve whichever IP appeared first in the
           filtered CSV above.  
         • The file must be pretty-printed with exactly four (4) space characters
           per indentation level and a final newline (Unix “\n”).  No extra
           whitespace is allowed after either the last key or the closing brace.

    Hints (do NOT reveal commands here):  
        • Standard Unix text-processing tools (e.g., awk/cut/grep) or Python
          oneliners are both valid approaches.  
        • Creating /home/user/analysis beforehand may be necessary.  

    When you have finished, the automated tests will:
        • Confirm that /home/user/analysis/invalid_password_attempts.csv exists
          and its content matches the specification byte-for-byte.  
        • Confirm that /home/user/analysis/invalid_password_summary.json exists
          and its content matches the required JSON structure and ordering.

    No additional output is required in the terminal beyond the usual command
    output; the presence and exact contents of the two files are what matters.

        bob,2023-08-21T12:06:02Z,192.168.1.45,invalid_password
        carol,2023-08-21T12:10:11Z,10.0.0.3,account_locked
        dan,2023-08-21T12:11:47Z,10.0.0.3,invalid_password
        erin,2023-08-21T12:12:33Z,192.168.1.100,invalid_password
        frank,2023-08-21T12:13:01Z,10.0.0.3,invalid_password

    Expected artefacts after correct completion:

         Content (with single trailing newline):
           user,timestamp,ip,reason
           alice,2023-08-21T12:05:34Z,192.168.1.45,invalid_password
           bob,2023-08-21T12:06:02Z,192.168.1.45,invalid_password
           dan,2023-08-21T12:11:47Z,10.0.0.3,invalid_password
           erin,2023-08-21T12:12:33Z,192.168.1.100,invalid_password
           frank,2023-08-21T12:13:01Z,10.0.0.3,invalid_password

      2) /home/user/analysis/invalid_password_summary.json  
         Content (with final newline):
           {
               "192.168.1.45": 2,
               "10.0.0.3": 2,
               "192.168.1.100": 1
           }

    Directory /home/user/analysis will not exist initially; the agent must create it.
